meaning: relative pronoun 'who, that, which' used with a qualified noun, masc. singular; (for the feminine, see 'anta; for the plural, see 'alla) the relative qualifier follows or precedes the qualified; e.g. ba'asi za-mas'a, or za-mas'a ba'asi 'the man who came'; it is also used without an antecedent, e.g. za-mota 'the one who died'; without an antecedent it can express nouns and adjectives; e.g. za-yakawwan 'future' (lit. 'that which will be'), za-yalahaq 'the elder', za-yazarra' 'the sower', za-yamawwat 'mortal'; negative adjectives 'in-, im-' are expressed by za- with a negative verb (e.g. za'iyyammawwat 'immortal'), or by za-+ 'albo with a noun (e.g. za-'albo fawsa 'incurable'); the element za- also expresses a genitive case relationship; e.g. ta'azaz qadāmāwi za-barhānāt 'the first ordnance of the lights' (for more details, see Dillmann 1907:326ff, 468ff); with the meaning 'of' za also occurs with names of materials, and in this case the structure serves as an adjective in English; e.g. 'aklil za-warq 'a golden crown' (lit. 'a crown, one of gold'). It appears also in expressions such as za-manfas 'spiritual' (lit. 'that one of spirit'), za-lams 'leper' (lit. 'that one of leprosy'); the element za-, fem. 'anta, is also used as a conjunction 'that, so that, because, since'; e.g. 'is it little for you za-harayakkamu that he has chosen you?'; 'thou hast saved thy life za-'aftanki warida in that thou hastened to come down'. Note that these sentences can also be translated by 'the fact that...'. the element za- is used with various prepositions: ba-za 'with which, at which, by which, through which'; diba-za 'upon whom, upon which'; za-'amanna, 'am-za 'from which'. Note also bo za- 'there is', bonu-za 'is there?'; 'affo za- 'how is it that?, what is it that?'; the repetition of za- in the form zazza- expresses the distributive; e.g. 'arnug za-warq zazzahalaq dalwatu 'earrings of gold, each an ounce in weight' (on the expression of the distributive, see Dillmann 1907:313-5); with possessive suffix pronouns the base is ziva-, for which see below. For more details on the structure of za-, see Dillmann 1907:332ff, 528ff, Dillmann 1029ff, Lambdin 106-7.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. di in alladi, also dialectally du, fem. datu, SAr. d, Mh. de, de, Soq. di, Heb. (occasionally) zu, ze, Aram. d, di, zi (see Jean-Hoftijzer 73), Syr.Md.Ug. d. For more details on the relative in Semitic, see Brockelmann 1908:324-6; 1913:563ff; Barth 1913:150ff; Pennacchietti Iff. ETHIOPIC: Tna. za, Har. zi. For the other languages, see Leslau 1956:65. Praetorius 1879:126 derives Amh. ya- 'of' from za.

meaning: 'this' (used as adjective and pronoun) both za and zā may be used as neuters; za---za 'this...that, one...another'; with prepositions: 'amza 'then, thereafter'; ka-maza 'such, like this, thus, in this way, so'; 'anbaynaza 'therefore'; baza 'there'; reinforced meanings in za-ssa, za-nu, za-ni, za-ke. For the meaning 'these', see 'allu, above. See also zantu, zakku, zaktu, zaya;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dā 'this' (trace of d in mud 'since then', from min-d(u), mun-d(u)), SAr. d-n, d-t, Mh. da,

meaning: 'this' (used as adjective and pronoun) both za and zā may be used as neuters; za---za 'this...that, one...another'; with prepositions: 'amza 'then, thereafter'; ka-maza 'such, like this, thus, in this way, so'; 'anbaynaza 'therefore'; baza 'there'; reinforced meanings in za-ssa, za-nu, za-ni, za-ke. For the meaning 'these', see 'allu, above. See also zantu, zakku, zaktu, zaya;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dā 'this' (trace of d in mud 'since then', from min-d(u), mun-d(u)), SAr. d-n, d-t, Mh. da,
From Ar. zabdīyya, zubdīyya 'of cream color (porcelain), bowl'; also in Amh. zabdāy 'small bowl'.

meaning: 'slaughter, sacrifice, offer sacrifices, (K*) twist, distort' (describes ways of slaughtering)
origin_explanation: zabāḥī, act. participle; zabūḥ 'slaughtered, sacrificed, offered'; zabḥ 'sacrifice'; zabḥat 'slaughtering (n), being slaughtered'; mazbaḥ (pl. mazābəḥ) 'beast for sacrifice, one who slaughters, means of slaughter'; mazbəḥ (K*) 'place where one slaughters'. SEMITIC: Ar. dabaha 'slaughter', SAr. dbh 'slaughter, sacrifice, slay', Heb. zəbaḥ 'slaughter', Ph. zbh, Ug. zbh, Akk. zebū. ETHIOPIC: Te. zäbḥa 'skin an animal'; for 'twist, distort', cp. Amh. azzäbba (from *zäbḥa).

zäbəl 'dung, excrement, scales of metal struck off by a hammer, (L) dregs, manure' (n); mazbəl (K*) 'spreading manure' (n); there seems to be confusion in the interpretation of this root. While B has zəbəl = Amh. fas 'axe', Sw. in D 1048 has zəbəl = Amh. fas 'breaking of wind' which comes close to zəbəl 'dung, excrement'. It would seem that the correct reading, rather than fas, and zəbəl does not mean 'iron or bronze tool' (Ludolf) or 'axe' (B). SEMITIC: Ar. zibl 'manure, dung of cattle', PbH. zebəl, Aram. ziblā, Syr. zeblā, Ug. zbl, Akk. ziblu 'refuse' (n). ETHIOPIC: Amh. zäbbälä 'flake off' (rust or oxidized metal), zäbällälä 'defecate copiously'. For 'excrement' and 'scales of metal struck off by a hammer', cp. Amh. ar 'excrement' and yäbrät ar 'the waste matter that is left after metal has been melted down from the ore', lit. 'the dung of the iron'.

(waḥabawomu zabānātīhomu 'they turned their backs, they went away', lit. 'they gave them their backs'); zabānāwī 'dorsal'; SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. zaban 'side, lateral part', Ar. (Yem) zabān 'abdomen'. Ludolf 657 compares Ar. zabārā 'dorsal'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. zaban 'back'; also in Cushitic: Sa. dabān 'back' (with alternation d:z).
zabārā 'strife, contention'.

zabərt 'fragment, piece'; mazbər, mazbār 'ruin, desert, desolate place, wilderness'; SEMITIC: Ar. zabara 'cut, prune' (Dozy), zubra 'fragment of iron', Ug. zbr 'prune'. Dozy I, 578 compares Heb. zāmar 'prune' (with alternation of labials). Dillmann 1049 (followed by Praetorius, ZDMG 53[1899].29, Nyberg 221, Conti Rossini 1931:258, against Barth 1893:50, n.1) identifies it with Semitic sbr, tbr. See also mazbara.
the meaning is probably reconstructed on the basis of Amh. zabbārā.

meaning: 'strike, smite, plague, beat, pluck (a musical instrument), beat (the drum), whip, scourge, chastise, shake, agitate, cut off, cast'
origin_explanation: azbata (Lt), causative; tazabəṭa, pass. of zabəṭa; tazabāṭa 'exchange blows, knock against one another'; yastazabāṭa, caus. of tazabāṭa; zabāṭt, act. part. of zabəṭa; also 'fighter'; zabūṭ (fem. zabəṭt, for zabəṭṭ), pass. part. of zabəṭa; zabəṭo 'hammered pattern, tinwork, beaten work, beaten (metal)'; zabṭat 'beating, whipping, punishment, plague, blow, stroke, scourge, beating or plucking (of a musical instrument)'; mazbaṭ 'striking, beating, mouthpiece (of a musical instrument), means of striking or beating'.

meaning: 'kick'. Dillmann 1050 compares the root šb (so also Barth 1893:50, Brockelmann 1908:163, with š, s > z by assimila- tion): Ar. šabț 'beat', Heb. šebet 'rod', Aram. šabṭā, Ar. šabaț 'smite with a rod', Syr. šabbel 'hammer' (v), Akk. šabāṭu 'hit'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. zäbṭa 'hit', Tna. zäbäṭä, šäbäṭṭ abälä 'hit lightly', Amh. zäbäṭṭäjä 'beat, thrash'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. zūfan; passed in Cushitic: Qem. zūfan.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. zäfär 'hem, fringe (of garment)', zäfār; metathesis in relation to zärf, below. The connection with Tna. zäffärä 'sich ausbreiten, wuchern (Pflanze)' suggested by Littmann-Höfner 506 is unlikely.
Ar. şafrā', fem. of 'aşfar 'yellow'. See also şafrā.

meaning: 'resin, pitch, bitumen'; zafata (T.M) 'coat with tar, mix ingredients for beer and allow to ferment' (denomina- tive).
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. zifț 'pitch', Aram. zifṭā (Fraenkel 151), Heb. zepeṭ, Syr. zeptā, zebṭā, Akk. zibṭu. ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh. zefț 'tar', Amh. zäfäṭä 'coat with tar, mix the ingredients for beer and allow to ferment'.

meaning: 'cause to ferment, im- merse in water'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. zäfzafä 'cause to ferment, immerse in water', Tna. zäfzafä 'soften by soaking', Amh. zäfzäfä 'cause to ferment, steep in water'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. zäff alä, same. Note that for the meaning of 'sprawling' the root zff (zaffa) is related to zfzf, 'anzafzafa.
SEMITIC: Ar. zaffa, zafzafa 'spread (wings)'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zäfzäf bälä 'undulate (a horse's mane)', Amh. anzäfäzzäfä 'shake, shiver, flap the wings'. See also zaffa.
ETHIOPIC: Te. zäba (zby) 'buy'; also in Cushitic: Bil.Kham. ğib.
from Amh. zäbiya 'haft (of axe, spear)'; also Tna. Gur. zäbiya, Har. zäbi 'wooden handle (of agricul- tural instruments)'; in Cushitic: Qem. zabi, Sa. zab, Af.Som. däb, Be. dabi (with d:z).

meaning: 'drive away, reject, hold back, (Y.M) annoy, trouble'; 'azabzaba (K*), causative;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dabba (dbb) 'remove, drive away', dabḍaba 'annoy' passing to 'drive back, repel, re- ject'. ETHIOPIC: Te. zäbzäba 'trouble, annoy', Amh. zäbzä- zäbä 'importune, harass'.

phonetics: zīfāl H-L (T.M), zäfāl H-L (Sw. in D 1339, under fāl)
origin_explanation: one recognizes fāl 'bean'; could zäfāl mean 'this (za) bean (fāl)'?

meaning: 'dance'; 'azfana, causative; zäfani 'dancer'; zäfän (zafn) 'dancing, choir, chant'; mäzfän 'place of dancing';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. zafana 'dance', Mh. zefōn. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zäfänä 'dance', Te. zäfna, Amh. zäfännä.

meaning: 'close in, seclude, plug up'; tazäga 'passive; tazäga'u (fem. zägä't) 'secluded, set apart'; secondary form of zagha, below.
'collect, gather, store, hide, hoard, accumulate, heap up'; 'azgaba (Lt), causative;

zagäbi (Gr.473), act. part. of zagaba; zägub, pass. part. of zagaba; mazgäb, mazgäb (pl. mazägäbt) 'treasure, treasury, wealth, storehouse, storeroom, magazine, granary, barn, container (for measuring wine)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Ar. kasaba 'acquire' suggested by Praetorius 1890:41 (followed by Brockelmann 1908:273) is not convincing. ETHIOPIC: Te. zäggäba 'collect, gather, amass', zägäbä, Amh. zäggäbä, Gur. mäzgäb 'register (Amharic).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. zägäba 'podocarpus', Amh. zägäba (for which see Strelcyn 1973:no.298). zägäba also Cushitic: Had. digiba (with alte d:z).

meaning: 'add a lot, add (wood to a fire)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. zäggädä, same.

meaning: 'prepare, make ready'; tazägädaya, (K*) tazägädaya 'prepare oneself, be ready, be prepared, decide together'; zagduy (K*) 'ready, prepared';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)zägädayä 'be ready, be pre- pared', Amh. (tä)zägäggä, Gur. (tä)zägägga; passed into Cushitic: Qua. zagäg.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. zaguf 'small flute', Amh. zaguf.

meaning: 'close, shut in, enclose, include'; 'uzgäha (K*), causative; tazägha, passive of zagha; zäguh (K*), act. part. of zagha; zäguh, pass. part. of zagha; zäguhawi 'who lives closed in, monk, cenobite'; zägḥat (n) 'closing, shutting in, enclosing'; mazgäḥ (pl. mazägäh, mazägäḥt) 'that by which anything is closed or shut (such as a clasp,
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Brockelmann 1927:28 compares it with Sem. skr. sgr (also Brockelmann 1928:475) > *zgr > zgh, the final h of which arose through 'Reim- wortbildung' with the opposite fth 'open'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)zäghe 'lose the voice after sing- ing a lot', lit. 'be closed (voice)', Amh. zägga 'shut, close up, enclose'. G. zägha is not to be compared with Te. dägha 'strike', as suggested by Littmann- Höfner 541. For Te. dägha, see G. däg'ha.

meaning: 'sea shell'; zag'ala (K*) 'resemble a sea shell' (denomina- tive);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh. zäg'al 'sea shell'; passed into Cush- itic: Qem. zäg'al.

meaning: 'hold in the hollow of the hand, seize a handful'; zägn (K*) 'handful, fistful'; mazgan, pl. mazägän (K*) 'means of taking a handful';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1067 compares it, with reserva- tions, with Ar. sagana 'imprison'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zägänä 'take a handful (of grain) and close the hand', Te. zägna, Amh. zäggänä, Gur. zägänä, Har. zägäna 'accumulate'.
from Amh. zägn; also Tna. zagni.
ETHIOPIC: Te. zägr 'broad lance', Amh. zägr 'long spear' (formerly carried by a monarch).

ETHIOPIC: Tna. zägrā 'guinea fowl', Te zägra, zäg- ra, Amh. zägra, zägra, zägra, Arg. zägra, Har. zikra, Gur. žägra; from Cushitic: Kam. zigre-ta, Bil. zägira, Sa. zägra, Af. dagrä (see also Cerulli 1938a: 89).

meaning: 'grow weak, become languid, be sluggish, be inactive, be indolent, soften, be

origin_explanation: SEM. Ar. (Dat) daḥal 'corrode, rust' ETHIOPIC. Te. zaḥal 'rust, dirt', Amh. corrosion' (from Geez). For 'be weak' zaʻalā 'become weak, be limp'. For 'be down', cp. Tna. zaḥalā 'cool off'. It is p the meaning 'cool down, cool off' does to this root. In this case there would be and zaḥla II.
FROM Ar. zaḥal; also in Amh. zuhal, Syr. zuhal.

meaning: be lukewarm, be warm, be hot'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC. Tna. zaḥamā 'be lukewarm', A 'be warm, be hot'.

meaning: be tranquil, be quiet, be calm, repose', causative, 'astazāḥana 'cause to be tranquil, calm'
origin_explanation: SEM. Ar. zaḥana 'be slow'. ETHIOPIC. Amh. (tā)zanā 'be tranquil, be at ease' related to zanna (root zn?) 'rest (v), be calm ease'.

meaning: peel off, decorticate, flay, husk, bark, skin, strip off (skin), (T) make a skin bag'
origin_explanation: SEM. Ar. dahiqa 'be peeled off'. ETHIOPIC. Tna. zahaqā 'strip off flesh from the bone', Amh. zaqā 'scrape clean, scrape a bare'.

meaning: ground where there is blood, cracks soil'
origin_explanation: the form is enigmatic; could it be composed of za-hermu?
ETHIOPIC. Tna. zatā 'threaten'.

meaning: be in excess, be in abundance, remain over, overflow, (K*) pour out, empty out, (Sw. in D 1039) drip'
origin_explanation: the basic meaning 'be abundant' developed into 'overflow, pour out, empty out'. SEMITIC. SAr. yhzh is from the root nzh 'provide irrigation' (so W. Müller and SabDic 102), and does not derive from zhh (as suggested by Biella 159). ETHIOPIC. Tna. zaḥzāhe 'cause to be numerous spill, drain', Amh. (tān)zazza 'be in excess', is žāzā 'be numerous'. The connection with zāḥzāḥa 'dip bread in sauce', suggested by Littm Höfner 494, is doubtful. See also zaḥaḥa, zāḥzāḥa.
ETHIOPIC. Tna. zəḥənən, zəḥnān 'powder made from the plant zəḥnān serving to dye red', Amh. zäkä nānā 'dye red using the plant zəḥnān'.

meaning: boast, be conceited, be haughty, glorify oneself, magnify oneself, be provoked, brawl, quarrel'
origin_explanation: SEM. Ar. zaḥar 'proud, boastful, arrogant, haughty, contentious, rebellious, quarrelsome'; zaḥurāwi 'vainglorious, given to vainglory'.

meaning: pride, arrogance, boastfulness, boasting, presumption, ambition, vainglory, insolence, strife, brawl ing'
origin_explanation: SEM. Ar. zaḥar 'proud, boastful, arrogant, haughty, contentious, rebellious, quarrelsome'; zaḥurāwi 'vainglorious, given to vainglory'.
ETHIOPIC. Tna. (tā)zāḥarā 'boast'. Te. gāḥara 'boast, brag', Amh. (tā)zārā.

SEM. Ar. daḥara 'put in store, reposit in secret'. ETHIOPIC. Te. zāḥara 'hide'. Littmann-Höfner 494 consider Te. zāḥara an Arabic loanword. Note, however, that normally Ar. h is represented as k in loanwords.

SEM. Ar. daḥara 'put in store, reposit in secret'. ETHIOPIC. Te. zāḥara 'hide'. Littmann-Höfner 494 consider Te. zāḥara an Arabic loanword. Note, however, that normally Ar. h is represented as k in loanwords.
in view of zaḥaḥa (above) it probably means 'meas ure' rather than 'camp'.

meaning: (acc. zakkʷa) m., (masc.) that (adj. pron.), the (person) concerned, the (point) in question'
origin_explanation: with inserted -s(s)a in zas(s)akku 'as for that' (so Dillmann 1056 and Rundgren 1955:33-4, but Praetorius 1890:26 interprets it as coming from za-asku). For the feminine, see əntakku; for the plural, see əllakku. See also zaktu; SEMITIC. for -k in the expression of distant objects, cp. Ar. dāka, dālika 'that' (for Arabic dialects, see Fischer 82ff), Mh. dāk 'that', Aram. dēk 'that', Syr. hallōk, Md. hak. For more details, see Brockelmann 1908:318ff; Barth 1913:80-3, 108ff, 115ff. ETHIOPIC. Gur. zax 'that', Amh. h in zih 'this' from k (see Praetorius 1879:123).
is the transcription of Heb. zkk (zākak). for the feminine, see ʾəntəkti; for the plural, see ʾəlləkʷtu, ʾəlləkʷtu. For -k, see zəkku; for -nu, see -tu.

meaning: 'remember, recollect, be mindful of, mention, remind, bring to memo- ry';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dakara 'remember, mention', SAr. dkr 'remember', Soq. dekr, Heb. zākar, Aram.Syr. dā- kar, ancient Aram. zkr, Md. zkr 'recite an incantation'. Ph. zkr, Akk. zakāru 'declare, speak, proclaim'. On the root zkr in Semitic, see W. Schot- troff, 'Gedenken' im Alten Orient und im Alten Testa- ment (1946). ETHIOPIC: Te. zäkra 'remember, recall'. Tna. zäkärä, Amh. (tä)zäkkärä, Gur. zäkkärä. For zäkär, cp. Te.Tna.Amh. täzkar, täskar.
from Ar. zukra 'small skin for wine or vinegar'.

phonetics: zaktu, zakʷtu (acc. zäkta, zəkʷta) ዝክቱ ዝኲቱ
meaning: 'that (adj., pron.), the (person) concerned, the (point) in question' (masc.);

origin_explanation: from Amh. zal. See also zahāl.

meaning: 'do con- stantly, do continuously, continue, perse- vere, perpetuate';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. zallafa 'disquiet, agitate'. Dillmann 1037 (followed by Brockelmann 1908:163) identifies it with Ar. talaba 'reproach' through regressive as- similation. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zäläfä 'reprimand, rebuke, blame', Amh. zällafä, Gur. zänäfä (for l:n, see Leslau 1979: 8.23).
SEMITIC: PbH. zālap 'pour', Aram.-Syr. zālap 'pour, drop, trickle down', related to dlpf; Ar. dalafa 'ooze through', Heb. dālap, Aram.-Syr. dālap.

SEMITIC: perhaps Syr. zalap 'decorate'. For the mean- ing of something precious or shining one could compare Ar. zalafa 'mother-of-pearl shell'. Or can there be any connection with zaluf of zalafa II?
is undoubtedly wrongly recorded instead of ʾizalluf 'not continuous, not perpetual' (for which see zlf unless it is to be connected with Amh. (tä)zäläfä 'be pliant, be flexible'.
SEMITIC: Ar. zalaga 'go at a gentle pace, glide'. PbH. zālag 'spill, pour out', Aram. zalag. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zäläggäg bälä, zäläggäg bälä 'flow', Amh. (tä)zläglägä.

meaning: 'be afflicted with sores, be afflicted with a disease (such as leprosy or elephantiasis)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1035 (followed by Stade 42, Hurwitz 52) derives it from a root zl, shp (Ar. salra 'be afflicted with leprosy') with a 'sufformative's (so Hurwitz), but a causative element -s does not occur as a suffix in Semitic. The derivation of zalgasa from za-lags going back to *rags (Ar. rigs 'dirt') or to nags (Ar. nigs 'dirty'), suggested by Rundgren 1955:183, is not convincing. Nor is the analysis of zlgs as z-lgs and the identification of lgs with G. qʾasl 'wound' (Ar. gls) with metathesis and q > g due to the voiced s, suggested by Praetorius 1890:23, convincing. ETHIOPIC: Amh. zälägg 'äsä 'be ill with a disease of the epidermis (leprosy, scabies)'.

meaning: 'drain out, draw, empty all the liquid from a vessel, (T.Y.TS. M) wring out a wet garment, squeeze';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dofar) mezelah 'vessel for drawing water', Aram.-Syr. zalah 'pour out', Md. zhl, zlh 'pour' (< zlh of Aram.-Syr.), Akk. salāhu 'sprinkle water' (Brockelmann 1928:197 following Zimmermann); for slh, cp. also Ug. slh, perhaps 'sprinkle'. Rabin in Cohen 1970:292, no. 11 connects it, with reserva- tions with Ar. dalaha, dalakha 'mix with water'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zälhe 'brew beer'. The root is related to zalala 1, below.

SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. dalla 'be low, base, abased, vile', Sh. eztelel 'become deranged' (W. Müller), Heb. zll (nif'al) 'treat cheaply', Syr. zal 'be of little value', Md. zll. ETHIOPIC: Amh. gällä (zälälä) 'be stupid', zolalla 'fool', (tän)zäläzzälä 'be dull, be stupid'.

meaning: 'wring a wet garment, strain a liquid, brew beer or mead';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. zällälä 'add water to beer, prepare grain for beer'. See also zalha, above.

SEMITIC: Ar. zalzala (zil) 'slip away, move', zalzala 'quake (earth)', Heb. zil (nif-'al) 'quake', Syr. zunzā- lā 'earthquake'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zälälä 'jump, leap', Amh.Gur. zäl- lälä, Arg. zällälä, Har. zäläla.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. zälläšä 'fell (a tree)'.

meaning: 'harmony, melody, tune, song, liturgical chant'; (habrata zemā 'harmony'); zemāwi (Gr.473) 'melodious'; zema (K*) 'produce a tune, sing' (denominative)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. zamzama 'resound, hum', Heb. zämam 'sound, buzz, murmur > ponder', Syr. zam (zmm) 'hum', zūmā 'humming of gnats'. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. zema 'song, mode of singing'.

meaning: 'blood, broth, (K) impurity left in broth by boiled meat'
origin_explanation: From Gk. zōmos 'soup, sauce, broth', Jud.-Aram. zōmē, zōmit 'broth', Syr. zūm.

meaning: (K*) 'associate, relate, connect'; 'azmada (K*), causative'; tazəmda 'be related'; tazämdä (Y) 'be related to one another, be akin to one another'; 'astazmädä 'establish a relationship'; zəmud (K*) 'related'; zamad (pl. 'azmädāt) 'kin, family, kins- man, related, relative, race, tribe, kind, sort, species'; 'azmäd (pl. of zamad) 'family, lineage, kinsmen, kinsfolk, relations, relatives, parents'
origin_explanation: (əzməd zašägä, əzməd šägäwəyän 'blood relatives'); tazmədat 'relationship'; təzmədd (for təzmədt) 'race, family, relationship, affinity, species, tribe'; təzməddawi (Gr.472) 'pertaining to race or family'; təzmädo (t) 'relationship'; təzməddənnā 'degree of relationship'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)zəmädä 'become related or a kinsman', Amh.Gur. (tä)zämmädä; Te. Gur. zämädd 'relative'; passed into Cushitic: Qua. zämäd.
'is probably to be identified with zamamə- lence' (see zamām, zamama, below).

meaning: 'nose rope of an animal, halter of woven cord, hook'; zamama (yəzməm) (K*) 'tie with a cord the lower jaw of a horse or mule', (Y.M) = Amh. gäffa 'do violence' (referring to the tying of the jaw of a horse with a cord)' (denominative)
origin_explanation: probably from Ar. zimām 'rein, halter' which, accord- ing to Noeldeke 1910:41, is of Aram.-Syriac (zə- mā) origin; also in Te. zəmam 'nose ring', Amh. zəmam, Tna. zämmamä 'bridle' (v); passed into Cushitic: Af. dumām, Som. dumām, Sa. zə- mama above.

meaning: 'rhythm of singing, accompaniment of religious songs with a stick, opening melody'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. zəmmama 'rhythm, swaying of a prayer stick in time to music'. See also zemā.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. zəmm alä 'be quiet', zəmmata (n).

zaman, zban, Akk. simānu. For the bibliography dealing with the origin of the root, as coming from Akk. simānu or Old Persian zamāna < Aram. zamān > Heb.Ar. Geez *zaman, see Wagner Kaufman 91-2 and n. 311. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. zämän, zäbän 'time', Gur. zämän, Har. zämän, Te.Arg. zäban; passed into Bil.Af. daban 'time' (with alternance d:z), Bil. demān.

meaning: 'sing, recite Psalms, musical instrument, celebrate with song, or glorify in song'; zəmmāri, act. part. of zammara; also 'psalmist who plays a musical instrument'; zəmmārē (pl. zəmmārəyāt) 'psalm, singing Psalms, hymn, collection of hymns'; zəmmārət 'praise'; mazammər 'church singer, psalmist, clerk of minor singers, musician'; mazmur (pl. mazmurāt, mazāmər, mazāmər) 'psalm, hymn, song, psaltery, chorus'; (mazmur za-däwit, mazmura däwit 'Psalms'; mazmər 'verse'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Noeldeke 1910:26 derives the Geez root from Aram.Syriac zəmar. In other Semitic lan- guages: Ar. zamara 'play upon a reed' (Lane), zimmer 'sing, praise', Md. zmar, Akk. zammāru. For Ar. zabūr 'Psalter', see Jeffery 148. ETHIOPIC: Te. zämmara 'sing, chant', Tna. zämmarä 'sing Psalms', Amh. zämmärä, Gur. zim- märä.

meaning: 'attest, expound, bear witness'; 'azmara 'proclaim solemnly, proclaim publicly, appeal, give testimony'; tazam (m)ara 'refer to someone as an authority, use as testimony, appeal to, appeal for justice, invoke, (MA) accuse'; M 266 wrongly renders Tayyā's tämattänä 'ap- peal' (Amharic translation of G. tazaman as 'be measured', taking it for tämattänä); zəmmāri, act. part. of zammara; zəmmur 'witness, authority, quoted as an au- thority'; məzmäär 'authority, witness'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. dmr 'initiate a legal process, give judgment'.

meaning: 'gather harvest'; 'azammara, 'azmara (K*) 'reap, gather harvest'; tazammara (K*) 'be gathered (harvest)'; denominative from 'azmarä, above.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. zämmärä, zämmärä 'begin', Arg. zämmärä, Gur. gämmärä; passed into Cushitic: Had. gämmärä-kko, Sid. gämmari. A connection between Amh. zämmärä and G. dämärä 'put in' suggested by Praetorius 1879:86, or with G. ('a)gma- ra 'complete, perfect', suggested by Ullendorff 1955:71, is unlikely. As for G. zammara 'begin', it is possible that K reconstructed it on the basis of Amh. zämmärä.

From Ar. zumurrud, zumurrud, of Persian origin; also Syr. zumrud, Aram. zəmōrad, 'izmōrad < Ar. zum- rud. According to Brockelmann, ZDMG 65 (1911). 146, n.1, the root goes back to zamaragd (see below) > zumurrud (by assimilation) > zumurrud.

from Gk. smaragdos 'emerald'; also PbH. 'izmā- ragd, Aram. zamargād, Syr. zamargədā (see Krauss 248); cp. also Ar. zabargad (for its being the same as or different from zumrud, see Lane 1251a). See also maragd, zamrud, above.

meaning: 'go on a military expedi- tion, plunder'; zamit 'military expedition'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. zämätä 'undertake a military expedi- tion', Te. zämta, Amh. zämmätä, Arg. zämmäta. Conti Rossini 1912:205 derives it from Cushitic: Qem. ğimāt 'raid', ğimi 'military expedition'.

meaning: 'fornicate, commit adultery, commit whoredom, have illicit intercourse'; 'azammawa, causative; also 'drive to fornica- tion'; zämmāwi (pl. zämmāwəyān) 'adulterer, fornica- tor, whoremonger';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Heb. zimmā (from zmm) 'loose conduct' (so Fraenkel, quoted by Praetorius 1890: 32, note*). Dillmann 1041 (followed by Noeldeke, ZDMG 40[1886].155, Brockelmann 1928:200, and M. Cohen, BSL 30[1929]144) compares it with zny (for which see zanaya), but not so W. Müller 1962:60. The connection with *adamm 'blameworthy' (used as a euphemism), suggested by Praetorius 1890:32, note*, is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zammāwā 'fornicate', Amh. zämma.
From Ar. zamāzima 'seers, fire worshipers'; also in Syr. məzamzəmane.

SEMITIC: Ar. danab 'tail', Soq. dinob, Mh. denōb, Heb. zānāb, Aram. danbā, Syr. dunbā, dubbāṭā, Md. zinbiṭa, zinipṭa, sinabṭa, Ug. dnb, Akk. zimbatu, zibbatu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zannab 'tail', Tna. zānāb, Amh. zālāb (from Geez).

SEMITIC: Ar. dubāb 'fly', Mh. debēb, Soq. edbiboh, Heb. zəbāb, Aram. dībabā, Md. dīdiba (pl.), Akk. zumbu, zunbu, zubbu. See for this root Noeldeke 1904:119-20. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Har. zambī 'fly', Amh.Arg.Gur. zamb, Gaf. zambā; from zbb (see Semitic, above) > znb > zmb. Geez zanb is probably taken from Amharic.

From Ar. zinbīr, pl. zanābir. In other Semitic languages: Mh. debēr 'hornet, bee', Heb. dəbōrā 'bee', Aram. zibbōrā, dibbōrā, Syr. zəbbūrā, zanbūrā, Md. zibura. See on this root Brockelmann 1908: 134, Blau 46, n. 3.
znf, tazānafa (K*) 'be uneven'. From Amh. tāzännāfä, same.

phonetics: (T) zanga'a 'be mad, be foolish, be crazy, be insane, be feeble-minded, be deviate, be diverted, be forgetful, tat at ran-tle rattle, rave, talk foolishly, talk loquacious'
meaning: 'be mad, be foolish, be crazy, be insane, be feeble-minded, be deviate, be diverted, be forgetful, tat at ran-tle rattle, rave, talk foolishly, talk loquacious'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1055 (followed by Rabin, El 9 [1969]. 154) connects it with Heb. šəg 'show madness'; cp. also Akk. šegū 'be furious'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zänga'e 'be feeble-minded, forget', Amh. zängāga, Gaf. zänāggā. W. Müller, ZDMG 131 (1981). 403 connects it, with reservations, with Gur. zänāggā (root zngy) 'feeble-minded, foolish, inept, mad, raving, one who forgets, less, heedless, demented, unconcerned'; suffix pronouns: e.g. zängu'ānihomu 'they (being) heedless'; zänga'e 'nonsense, delirium, senselessness, sanity, stupidity, cause of alienation'.
SEMITIC: Dillmann 1056 (followed by Stade 25) connects it with Ar. zagzağa 'scoff at'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. zəng°ag°e 'mockery, veiled insult' (from Geez).

phonetics: (yaznəm) 'rain' (v.); uznama 'cause to rain, send rain'; tazannama 'be irrigated by rain'; zənām 'rain, rainy season'
meaning: 'rain' (v.); uznama 'cause to rain, send rain'; tazannama 'be irrigated by rain'; zənām 'rain, rainy season'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. zariba 'flow' (with alternance of liquids m:b), Sar. dnm 'rain', Heb. zərem [Barth 1893:43-4), Akk. zanānu 'rain', nu 'rain' (n). Praetorius, ZDMG 72 (19) connects the G. root with Ar. (Dat) muzn 'pluie torrentielle' > znm in Geez. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zänābā, zänāmā 'to rain', Te. zänma, Amh. zänāba, zänāmā, Arg. zänāba, zālāma, Gur. zänābā; 'rain' (n.): Tna.Amh. nab, Te. zəlam, Gaf. zanab°ā, Har. zənāb, zənab, zərāb, zəlam. Reinisch 1887:312 (followed by Cohen 1947:no.299) compares Bil. zuwā, zōwā < *zanwā.

phonetics: 'stone attached to the front of a bullock' (to prevent him from running away)
meaning: 'stone attached to the front of a bullock' (to prevent him from running away)
origin_explanation: from Amh. zänana, same.

phonetics: (MA) 'be crafty, be sly'; zanapi (MA 302) 'crafty, sly' (should be pi)
meaning: 'be crafty, be sly'; zanapi (MA 302) 'crafty, sly' (should be pi)'

origin_explanation: From Amh. zänäqä, same.

phonetics: 'belt, broad colored girdle that holds the qamis together' (for more details see Hammerschmidt 1970:153)
meaning: 'belt, broad colored girdle that holds the qamis together'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. zunnār 'belt' (for which see 1845:196-8) < Aram. zōnārā 'belt', Syr. zōnārā worn by monks and priests'. For its Greek (zōna-rion) origin, see Krauss 245. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. zənnar 'cartridge belt', Har. zännār.

phonetics: zəntu-zəntu 'this...that, one...another'; zəntu-zəkku (zəktu) 'this...that'; bəzənta zəntu 'because of this, therefore'; ənəbayna (babayna) zəntu 'because of this, therefore'; bazəntu 'here'; for the feminine, see zātti; for the plural, see ǝllontu.
meaning: 'this...that, one...another'; zəntu-zəkku (zəktu) 'this...that'; bəzənta zəntu 'because of this, therefore'; ənəbayna (babayna) zəntu 'because of this, therefore'; bazəntu 'here'; for the feminine, see zātti; for the plural, see ǝllontu.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Maltese) dānā 'this' (see Fischer 67ff who does not connect this -n with that of the other Semitic languages mentioned below), Sar. dn, Hars. den, Aram. dənā, dn, zn, znh, zn-k (Barth 1913:106, 107; Jean-Hoftijzer 78-9), Ph. zn. For the n in G. zəntu, see Rundgren 1955:301, 362, index, under zəntu. For the final -tu, see -tu, above.

phonetics: (Y.M) 'inform, tell, announce, bring tidings, declare, narrate, proclaim, relate, report, make known, make inquiries'; tazenawa, passive; also 'receive news'; tazəyāwawa, tazayāwana (K*) tazəyāwana 'an-nounce to one another, recount to one another, talk together, make inquiries of one another, tell each other'; 'astazəyāwana (Gr.437), 'astazəyāwana (K*), caus. of tazəyāwana; zenāwī 'one who brings news, announcer, teller, chronicler, historian, evangelist'; zənāwī (K*) 'told, informed, announced'; zenā 'information, announcement, rumor, re-port, news, narrative, message, tidings, story, history, account, pronouncement, fame, reputa-tion, repute'; (zenā mawā'əl 'Book of Chronicles'); mazənəw, (Gr) mazənəwī 'announcer, messen-ger'; mastazəyāwəna (Lt) 'historian'.
meaning: 'inform, tell, announce, bring tidings, declare, narrate, proclaim, relate, report, make known, make inquiries'; tazenawa, passive; also 'receive news'; tazəyāwawa, tazayāwana (K*) tazəyāwana 'an-nounce to one another, recount to one another, talk together, make inquiries of one another, tell each other'; 'astazəyāwana (Gr.437), 'astazəyāwana (K*), caus. of tazəyāwana; zenāwī 'one who brings news, announcer, teller, chronicler, historian, evangelist'; zənāwī (K*) 'told, informed, announced'; zenā 'information, announcement, rumor, re-port, news, narrative, message, tidings, story, history, account, pronouncement, fame, reputa-tion, repute'; (zenā mawā'əl 'Book of Chronicles'); mazənəw, (Gr) mazənəwī 'announcer, messen-ger'; mastazəyāwəna (Lt) 'historian'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the derivation from Sem. dn 'ear' sug-gested by Dillmann 1063 is unlikely. Praetorius 1890:33 (followed by Brockelmann 1908:169) iden-tifies it with Ar. tannā (my) 'repeat, tell'. Barth 1893:51 (followed by Buhl 850) adds Heb. šānā 'do for a second time' to which Md. tna and Akk. šanū 'repeat' should be added. If this were so, G. zenawa would have to go back to *senawa with s-n > z-n by assimilation.

SEMITIC zny. Ar. zanā (zny) 'fornicate', Heb. zānā, Aram.-Syr. zanā, Md. zna. Dillmann 1055 identifies it with zammawa, above. ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh. zānnā 'commit adultery, fornicate'; passed into Cushitic: Af. sannaw, Sa. zanaw. See also zanawa.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. zānzāriṭu 'subdeacon'; for the ending -iṭu, see also galametu.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. zabbā 'ebony'; Amh. zopi; also in Cushitic: Qua. zōpi.
perhaps corruption of Gk. hypodiakonos 'subdeacon' with the Geez particle za (W. Müller).

meaning: Amh. mälämmälä 'choose, set apart, prune, cut down, cut the lower branches'

origin_explanation: possibly from Ar. ziqq 'skin (for a receptacle)'; also in Amh. zaq. For Ar. ziqq < Aramaic, see Fraenkel 171; Akk. ziqq 'wineskin' is considered an Aramaic loanword (CAD, vol. 21, p. 129 contrary to Zim-mern 34 who considers Aram. ziqqā an Akkadian loanword). The connection with Heb. zaq 'filter' suggested by Koehler 264 is unlikely.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. ziq 'a short piece of a hymn in honor of a saint to be chanted during commemo-ration of the saint' (see Getatchew 1979:154).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. zaqʿanā 'become a deacon', zaqʿanā 'appoint a deacon'; variant of diyāqon, above (with alter-nance d:z). See also zəyāqʿana.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. zaqq bālā 'lower oneself', alā 'decline, be low, sink (price)'.

`azrəʿa, (K*) `azrəʿa, causative; tazara 'be sown, bear seeds, be spring up'; zarāʾi 'who seeds, sower'; zərʿu 'seeded'; zar (pl. `azrəʾt) 'seed, seedling, plantation, offspring, progeny, race, lineage'; zərʿat, (K*) zərʿat 'seeding, sowing'; mazrāʿ (T), mazrāʿ (L), pl. mazārəʿ 'basket, container (for measuring cereals)'; SEMITIC: Ar. darāʾa, zarāʾa 'sow, seed', SAr. m-dr-ʾi 'sown field', Heb. zēraʿ 'sow', Aram. dərāʿ, Syr. zərāʿ, Md. zra, Ug. drʿ, Akk. zēru 'offspring'. J. Aro, ZDMG 113 (1963), 476 states, with reservations, that Ar. Aram. zrʿ are Canaanite loanwords, but see Blau 48, n. 9. ETHIOPIC: Te. zäraʿ 'seed, sow', Tna. zärʿe, Gur. zärra, Har. zärāwa, Gaf. zärra; passed into Cushitic: Aw. zāru-x°a. See also zarawa, zaraya, zarzara.

meaning: 'hit, strike the strings of a musical instrument, (K*) kick, trample'
origin_explanation: zarb, (B) zarab 'a stick for playing a stringed instrument'; zarba lassān (Lt) 'of forceful tongue'; mazrab 'hammer'; SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) madrab 'club, thick stick', relat-ed to Ar. daraba 'hit, play a musical instrument'. For G. zarba lassān, cp. Ar. dariba 'be sharp' in the expression dariba lisāmuhu 'his tongue was sharp' (either eloquent or clamorous), for which see Lane 958. ETHIOPIC: Amh. zärb 'anvil'.
perhaps from zaraba 'hit, bite' (referring to pepper).
ETHIOPIC: Amh. zäräbbäbä 'spread out'; perhaps also Tna. zärbāb 'be pulled, be dragged'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. zärbedo, same (from Geez).
from Byzantine Gk. serbouIon 'shoe of undressed leather', through Ar. zarbūn, zarbūl.

meaning: 'carpet, tapestry, rug, (OT) coverlet for a bed'
origin_explanation: zarbata (T.Y.M) 'spread a carpet' (denomina-tive); from Ar. zurbiyya 'carpet' (for which see Fraenkel 92, Noeldeke 1910:53). Reinisch 1902:118 compares Som. dirmo (with alternance d:z and m:b).
one recognizes depa 'lie in ambush' (for which see dyp, above).
see zärāp, below.

`azrafa, causative; zarāfi 'raider'; zarāf (K*) 'raider'; zarāfā 'pillage, plundering, raid'; SEMITIC: Ar. zarrafa 'carry away', SAr. zrf-t 'party, troop of men', Syr. zərīptā 'attack, assault'. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. zärräfā 'pillage, raid', zärfa, Arg. zärāfa, Har. zärāfa; passed into Cushitic: Kam. zäräffo, Qem. zärāf. Aw. zäräf-x°a.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. zärfi, zärf 'hem', Amh. zärf, zafar.

meaning: 'be well-dressed, be deco-rated, be adorned, decorate, adorn'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. zärgāfā 'comb the hair', Amh. zärägāfā 'make silver or gold laces'.

meaning: 'sprinkle, spray, wring out a wet garment'
origin_explanation: zəruh, zəruh (T.Sw. in D 1397), pass. participle; ETHIOPIC: Amh. (a)zärra 'sprinkle, spray'.

meaning: 'rend (with the teeth), tear open, strew about'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: related to Amh. zäräkkäkā 'tear open', whose final k may come from a noun *zarkāt (from zrk).
perhaps a corruption of Gk. skorpios 'scorpion' (W. Müller).

meaning: 'calumniate, reproach, revile, (Y) mock, insult, make pointed remarks'; (Y) = Amh. säggäddälä 'bow down in worship' (is strange)
origin_explanation: `azarkaya (K*), causative; tazarkaya, pass. of zarkaya; zarkāyi, act. part. of zarkaya; zarkuy (K*), pass. part. of zarkaya; zarkāye 'calumny, reproach, insult'; zarkayannā, same; ETHIOPIC: perhaps related to Gur. zəngäggʿa, root zngy, zrgy 'speak, talk'; G. zarkaya would then mean 'talk badly about someone'. The connection with Te. zärka (zrky) 'thunder with a long peal, rejoice' suggested by Littmann-Höfner 496 is doubtful.
zərmā (Gr.437) 'messenger'.
perhaps a misprint for zardāp, in view of zardapa II 'bake bread'.

meaning: = Amh. zəran, tərən 'small drum kept beside a large one and used for summoning troops or calling people to a ban-quet'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. zaraqa 'shoot with a javelin', mizraq 'javelin'. Mh. zerəq 'sting, stab'. Heb. zəraq 'toss, throw'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zäräqa 'pierce with a spear', mäzraq 'javelin'. Te. märzaq (with metathesis) 'big staff with iron point'; perhaps also Amh. mäzraq 'spear which has a square tip' (with alternance of glottalized q:t).
ETHIOPIC: Amh. zärq 'joke, jest' (n.), from Geez. Tna. zäqqära 'joke, tease' (with metathesis).
probably a misprint for zärät, below.

meaning: 'divulge a secret, empty out the contents of a sack'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Mh. der 'spread out', Sh. derr. ETHIOPIC: Amh. zärrarä 'spread out to dry'. See also zarzara.

meaning: = Amh. zärär 'a parasitic plant that grows in the fields and serves as food in times of need'.

origin_explanation: a misprint for zawasa, below, or the other way around.

SEMITIC: a suggested connection with Ar. zarāfa 'giraffe' is enigmatic (Noeldeke 1910:57). ETHIOPIC: Tna. gärat 'giraffe'; probably not to be connected with Amh. gərattä qäččən 'giraffe', lit. 'thin-tailed'. See on this root Hommel 230, 393, and zäräq, above. Reinisch. WZKM 10 (1896). 357 derives it from Cushitic: Kham. ğərä, Sa.Af. garä.
for various interpretations, see Hommel 1887. Conti Rossini states in RSE 10 (1951), 43, n.2 `pianta imprecisata, il nome zerṭanē brandə derive da cattiva lettura`.

meaning: 'scatter, spread, dissipate, disperse, waste, squander, lavish, dis- tribute, divide'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. darā (dry) 'winnow, scatter', Heb. zərw (zərwy)y, Aram.-Syr. dərā, Md. dra, Ug. dry, Akk. zarū. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zäräwä 'scatter', (a)zräwä (a)yä 'winnow', Amh. (a)zärra 'winnow, scatter'. Tna. zäräq 'a 'sift' with a repeated z as 3rd radical. Te. possibly zära (zərw)y 'flow'. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. zaraw 'winnow'. See also zaraya, zarzara, zara.
see zarawa.

meaning: 'chain-work interlaced in the form of a net, mesh, sieve'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Amh. zärzäk 'lattice work of wood or metal'; zarzeqa in the meaning 'sift' comes close to Tna. zäräq 'a 'sift' with a repeated z as 3rd radical.

meaning: 'disperse, scatter, tear apart, lacerate, crush, dishevel (hair), shatter, smash, dissolve, bring to naught'.

origin_explanation: possibly from zän -ti. -ti being the feminine marker. For the masculine, see zəntu, zä.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. zättärä (with i) 'pour out roughly or brusquely'. For the variants t:t, cp. Amh. zätär, zätär 'pumpkin'.
from Amh. zäwe 'a creeper'; perhaps to be iden- tified with Te. zäwwə'o 'kind of tree', Tna. zawa.

meaning: 'entertain oneself, enjoy oneself, converse, meditate (talk to oneself), nonsense, talk idly, jest, be wanton, be impudent'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. *zyy, zəy 'shout, bark at' (Baum- gartner 265 quoting Dahood), Ug. zġ, zġw 'make a sound' (Gordon 393). ETHIOPIC: Te. təzawə'a 'entertain oneself', 'speech, language'. perhaps Amh. (aw)wazza 'amuse- entertain' from wz? (metathesis in relation to zw). Reinisch 1895:207 compares Be. śōy 'story, report, news'.
SEMITIC: Heb.Aram.Syr. zə'a (zə'w) 'tremble', Md. zha (zuh), Ar. za'za'a 'make tremble, agitate'. It is possible that K. simply transcribes Heb. zə'a, ze'a. ETHIOPIC: Tna. zə'a bä'lä 'perspire'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. zäwdi 'crown'. Te. zäwdi (from Tna.), Amh. zäwd; passed into Cushitic: Bil. zawad, Qua. zaüd.
SEMITIC: Ar. zəfəfa (zyf) 'walk in a proud and haughty way'. ETHIOPIC: Te. zäf wäda 'boast'. See also wəzafa, təwzəft.
From Ar. zawg, zawg, of Greek (zeugos) origin; also Aram.-Syr. zawgā 'equal pair', Pbh. zūg, Ar. zawg.

meaning: ETHIOPIC: Tna. zawāgā 'form a group', Amh. zawwāgā 'form a group or clique, make a pair or couple', zāwg 'group, couple'.
origin_explanation: (Fraenkel 106, Noeldeke 1910:44, Jeffery 155). For the Aramaic origin of Ar. zawg, see also A. Fischer 1953:51. Note that Md. zawa, and Ar. zaww 'couple, pair', are identified by Noeldeke 1875:41, n.6 with zwg, zwg.
From Ar. gawhar (of Persian origin); also Te. gāwhar. See also zawārāh.

meaning: = Amh. aqallāmā 'dye, color, prepare thread for the shuttle by winding it on a reel'. Note that MA renders it by 'préparer de l'encre, teindre'.

origin_explanation: zawwara (K) 'turn around' (tr); 'azora 'make turn'; the reading FØM-CØ- yā-'awwaromu 'it makes them blind' is more appropriate (so in Sirach, Addis Ababa edition) than FØM-CØ- yāzawwaromu, as given in Dillmann 1060; tazāwwara (T) 'go back and forth'; zaw(w)āri (K*) act. part. of zora, zawwara; zawr 'circle, circular course, environs'; SEMITIC: Ar. zāra 'visit' (Müller 1962:61), Aram. zār; possibly Heb. zār (zwr) 'turn aside from' conditioned by the preposition min 'from' (without min 'from', the verb zār probably has the meaning 'visit'). Md. zur 'visit', Akk. zāru 'twist, roll, turn'. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. zorā 'go around, turn around', Te.Arg. zora, Gaf. zārā; passed into Cushitic: Aw. zura-g'a, Sa. zār. The root is related to Te. dora 'go around' (with alternance d:z).

meaning: 'endure, be permanent, remain, (Y.KG.M) guard, protect, hide'
origin_explanation: tazawwara 'endure, remain, last, (T.M) be guarded'; Dillmann 1059 seems to connect it with zwr, above.
see zawhər, above.
should be zawāriq HØL, borrowed from riq (pl. of zarrāqa) 'an instrument made of copper or brass for shooting naphtha into a besieged place' (for which see Lane 1227).

meaning: 'cram bread into the mouth, eat voraciously'
origin_explanation: see zarasa, above.

meaning: name of the letter zāy, name of God
origin_explanation: For the symbolic interpretation of the names of the letters as the names of God, see also te.
from Amh. zay.

meaning: 'here, on the spot, hither, to this place'
origin_explanation: bazaya 'here'; 'əmzaya, 'əmannazaya 'hence, from this place, from here'; 'əsəka zaya 'up to here, hitherto'; haba zaya 'up to here'; ETHIOPIC: Gur. ziya 'here', tā--ziyya 'toward here', -(y)ye, directional 'toward'. Barth 1913:90 compares y of zaya with y of ya'aze 'now' and considers -ya a demonstrative which he compares with -y of various Semitic languages. Note that in H£ the y is not geminated (thus zaya) whereas in H£ it is geminated (thus hayya).

ETHIOPIC: Tna. zega 'subject, tributary', Amh. zegā 'be poor', zega 'poor person, client', Har. zega 'poor', Gur. zə'āgā 'become poor', zega 'poor', from Cushitic: Qab. zēga 'poor', Bil. žiga, Or. dēgā.

zayyəna (K*) 'cut hair'; zayyāni (K*) 'barber'; məzayyən 'barber'; from Ar. zāna (zyn) 'adorn, embellish'; also in Amh. zayyānā 'be beautiful'. In other Semitic languages: Soq. zen 'decorate', Akk. za'ānu, zānu.

meaning: 'become a deacon' (denominal); the form is strange
origin_explanation: zəyāqon (T), zəyāq°an (K*) 'deacon'; see zeq°ana.

('ada zayt 'olive tree'); ('oma zayt 'olive tree'); ('asāda zayt 'olive grove'); (qəb'a zayt 'olive oil'); from Ar. zayt 'olive oil' (for its Aramaic origin, see Fraenkel 148, Noeldeke 1910:42); also in Amh. Har. zayt, Tna. zāyti, Te. zet. In the other Semitic languages: Soq. zeyt 'olive oil', Heb. zayit, Aram. zītā, Syr. zaytā, Md. zaita, Ug. zyt, zt, Ph. zyt. See also zayton, zayton.
(əlatə 'əhud zayton 'Thursday after the fast of Nineveh').
from Ar. zaytūn (of "foreign" origin; see Fraenkel 147, following Guidi); also Amh. zäytūn. See also zayton.
see zayton.
seems to be onomatopoetic.
from Ar. zīğ (of Persian origin); also Syr. zīg.
from Ar. ('al)ğādy (for which see Kunitzsch 1961:62).
from Ar. ('al)ğawzā' (for which see Kunitzsch 1961:23-4).
